meanings.definition: A period of isolation imposed on people or animals to prevent the spread of contagious disease.
examples: Travelers from the affected region were placed in quarantine.
The sick passengers were kept in quarantine for two weeks.

meanings.definition: To place in isolation to stop disease from spreading.
examples: Officials quarantined the infected livestock.

meanings.definition: To give a clear, visible, or concrete form to an idea, quality, or feeling.
examples: She embodies the spirit of the team.
The new law embodies years of careful reform.

meanings.definition: To include or contain something as part of a whole.
examples: The report embodies all the findings of the survey.

meanings.definition: To set free from slavery or bondage.
examples: The owner chose to manumit his slaves in his will.
Several captives were manumitted after the war.

meanings.definition: To reduce something to the smallest possible amount or degree.
examples: Careful planning helped minimize the risk of error.
Wear a helmet to minimize the chance of injury.

meanings.definition: To make something seem smaller or less important than it is.
examples: He tried to minimize his role in the failure.

meanings.definition: A person who takes part in a conversation or discussion.
examples: The diplomat was a skilled interlocutor in the talks.
Her interlocutor nodded thoughtfully at each point.

meanings.definition: Having a large number of people living there.
examples: Tokyo is one of the most populous cities in the world.
They moved from a quiet village to a populous metropolis.

meanings.definition: A legal right to keep or claim someone's property until a debt owed is paid.
examples: The bank placed a lien on the house until the loan was repaid.
Contractors can file a lien if they aren't paid for their work.

meanings.definition: To strike or push with the head or horns.
examples: The goat butted the fence in frustration.
The ram butted its rival to defend the herd.

meanings.definition: To join or place end to end without overlapping.
examples: The two boards butt together to form a smooth surface.

meanings.definition: A clever trick or scheme used to deceive or persuade someone.
examples: She used every wile to get what she wanted.
He saw through the salesman's wiles.

meanings.definition: Easily made angry; quick-tempered.
examples: The irascible old man shouted at anyone who crossed his lawn.
Hunger made him irascible and short with his coworkers.

meanings.definition: The act of buying back or recovering something, such as a pledged or mortgaged item.
examples: The redemption of the loan freed the family's land.
He saved for years for the redemption of the pawned watch.

meanings.definition: The act of being saved from sin, error, or evil.
examples: The story is about a criminal's path to redemption.

meanings.definition: A mistake in the use of language, or a breach of good manners or accepted custom.
examples: His essay was marred by several grammatical solecisms.
Wearing jeans to the formal dinner was a social solecism.

meanings.definition: Failing to give proper care or attention to something.
examples: He was neglectful of his duties and missed deadlines.
Neglectful owners left the garden to fall into ruin.

meanings.definition: To secure a rope by winding it around a cleat or pin.
examples: The sailor belayed the line to keep the sail in place.
She belayed the rope firmly before the climber descended.

meanings.definition: To stop or cancel an order (used as a command).
examples: Belay that order and stand down.

meanings.definition: Lacking compassion; extremely cruel or brutal.
examples: The prisoners were kept in inhuman conditions.
Such inhuman treatment shocked the whole nation.

meanings.definition: A reduction or decrease in size, amount, or importance.
examples: There was a steady diminution in the number of visitors.
The drug caused a diminution of pain within minutes.

meanings.definition: To deliberately tell a lie after swearing to tell the truth.
examples: He perjured himself by lying under oath.
She refused to perjure herself to protect a friend.

meanings.definition: To set right or make up for a wrong, often through compensation or correction.
examples: The court acted to redress the injustice.
They sought to redress the balance between rich and poor.

meanings.definition: Compensation or remedy for a wrong or grievance.
examples: The victims demanded redress for their losses.

meanings.definition: A person who hoards money and spends as little as possible.
examples: The old miser refused to heat his house to save money.
Despite his wealth, he lived like a miser.

meanings.definition: Able to be grasped or seized.
examples: The branch was just prehensible from where he stood.
Only the lowest, prehensible fruit could be picked by hand.

meanings.definition: Happening in irregular bursts; intermittent and not steady.
examples: She got only a few hours of fitful sleep before the exam.
The fire gave off a fitful, flickering light.

meanings.definition: One of three people who share authority or rule jointly in public office.
examples: Caesar served as a triumvir alongside Pompey and Crassus.
Each triumvir distrusted the ambitions of the other two.

meanings.definition: Any person or power that opposes or sets itself against Christ.
examples: Some preachers branded the tyrant an Antichrist.
Medieval writers debated when the Antichrist would appear.

meanings.definition: Without prior thought or preparation; on the spur of the moment.
examples: I can't tell you offhand how many people attended.
He couldn't recall the figure offhand and had to check his notes.

meanings.definition: Casual or curt in a way that suggests indifference.
examples: Her offhand reply made him feel unwelcome.

meanings.definition: Faithful loyalty, especially that owed by a vassal to a lord.
examples: The knight swore fealty to his king.
They pledged their fealty in a solemn ceremony.

meanings.definition: The person to whom something is given or promised as security.
examples: As pledgee, the bank held the deed until the loan was repaid.
The pledgee may sell the collateral if the debtor defaults.

meanings.definition: The act or power of moving from one place to another.
examples: Fish use their fins for locomotion through the water.
The injury affected his locomotion for several weeks.

meanings.definition: Producing no useful result; pointless.
examples: It was futile to argue with someone so stubborn.
All their futile attempts to start the engine ended in failure.

meanings.definition: The state of not being enough or adequate for a purpose.
examples: The crops failed because of an insufficiency of rain.
An insufficiency of funds forced the project to close.

meanings.definition: Happening as if through divine guidance; fortunate or opportune.
examples: Their providential arrival saved the stranded hikers.
It seemed providential that the rain stopped just before the wedding.

meanings.definition: Causing harm in a gradual, subtle way that is hard to notice.
examples: The insidious disease spread for years before symptoms appeared.
Propaganda has an insidious effect on public opinion.

meanings.definition: Showing careful and persistent effort or dedication.
examples: Her sedulous study paid off with top marks.
He kept sedulous notes on every experiment.

meanings.definition: In Greek myth, a beautiful youth who fell in love with his own reflection.
examples: Like Narcissus, he could not stop admiring himself in the mirror.
The painting depicts Narcissus gazing into the pool.

meanings.definition: A spring-flowering bulb plant such as the daffodil.
examples: Rows of narcissus bloomed along the garden path.

meanings.definition: Something within which something else originates, develops, or is contained.
examples: The family is often called the matrix of a child's values.
Fossils were embedded in a matrix of solid rock.

meanings.definition: A rectangular array of numbers or symbols arranged in rows and columns.
examples: The students learned to multiply one matrix by another.

meanings.definition: Boldly rude or disrespectful.
examples: The insolent student talked back to the teacher.
His insolent tone offended everyone in the room.

meanings.definition: Loss of the ability to move or feel in part of the body.
examples: The stroke left him with paralysis on his left side.
Paralysis of the legs confined her to a wheelchair.

meanings.definition: A state of total inability to act or function.
examples: The strike caused a complete paralysis of the transport system.

meanings.definition: To make mocking or scornful remarks.
examples: The crowd gibed at the losing team as it left the field.
Rivals gibed about his clumsy mistake for weeks.

meanings.definition: The violent seizing and carrying off of property, as in war.
examples: The invading army left a trail of rapine and ruin.
Villages were abandoned to fire and rapine.

meanings.definition: A fusible metal alloy used to join or repair metal surfaces.
examples: He melted the solder to fix the broken wire.
A thin line of solder held the two pipes together.

meanings.definition: To join or mend metal pieces using this alloy.
examples: She soldered the components onto the circuit board.

meanings.definition: Feeling worried or uneasy about something uncertain.
examples: She felt anxious while waiting for the test results.
He grew anxious as the deadline drew near.

meanings.definition: Eagerly wanting something to happen.
examples: They were anxious to begin their long-awaited vacation.

meanings.definition: In Roman myth, a woodland deity that is part human and part goat.
examples: A faun played its pipes among the trees.
The statue portrayed a faun with curling horns.

meanings.definition: Involving or characterized by much sitting and little physical activity.
examples: A sedentary lifestyle can lead to health problems.
Her sedentary job kept her at a desk all day.

meanings.definition: Hidden beyond what is openly stated or shown; concealed.
examples: Her generosity hid an ulterior motive.
They suspected he had ulterior reasons for helping.

meanings.definition: Coming at a later time; subsequent.
examples: The plan's ulterior stages would be decided later.

meanings.definition: A deadly, widespread epidemic disease.
examples: The pestilence swept through the city, killing thousands.
Whole regions were emptied by war and pestilence.

meanings.definition: The ceremony of formally making someone a priest or minister.
examples: Her ordination took place in the cathedral.
After years of study, he awaited his ordination.

meanings.definition: Happening too soon or at an unsuitable time.
examples: His untimely death shocked the whole community.
The untimely interruption ruined the surprise.

meanings.definition: Having qualities traditionally regarded as womanish in a man.
examples: His critics mocked his effeminate manners.
The character was written as soft and effeminate.

meanings.definition: Relating to the body.
examples: The patient suffered no lasting bodily harm.
Exercise improves both bodily and mental health.

meanings.definition: Cheerful and prompt willingness to do something.
examples: She accepted the invitation with alacrity.
The recruits obeyed the order with surprising alacrity.

meanings.definition: The branch of physics dealing with the motion of fluids and the forces on them.
examples: Hydrodynamics explains how ships move through water.
Engineers used hydrodynamics to design the dam's spillway.

meanings.definition: An institution for higher learning or specialized training.
examples: She enrolled in a military academy after high school.
The academy is known for its excellent music program.

meanings.definition: A society of distinguished scholars, artists, or scientists.
examples: He was elected to the national academy of sciences.

meanings.definition: To deprive someone of an inheritance they would otherwise receive.
examples: He threatened to disinherit his son after the quarrel.
She was disinherited and left without a penny.

meanings.definition: Small in degree, amount, or importance.
examples: There was a slight delay in the schedule.
He felt a slight pain in his ankle.

meanings.definition: Slender and delicate in build.
examples: She was a slight figure in a long coat.

meanings.definition: Fond of company; sociable.
examples: His gregarious nature made him popular at parties.
She was too gregarious to enjoy working alone.

meanings.definition: Of animals, living in groups or herds.
examples: Elephants are gregarious animals that travel in herds.

meanings.definition: To overwhelm with a great flood of water.
examples: The storm deluged the streets within minutes.
Floodwaters deluged the low-lying farms.

meanings.definition: To overwhelm with a very large quantity of something.
examples: The office was deluged with complaints after the change.

meanings.definition: Marking or relating to the beginning of something.
examples: The inceptive stage of the disease is easy to overlook.
Some verbs have an inceptive form meaning 'to begin to.'

meanings.definition: Lasting or recurring throughout the year or for many years.
examples: Poverty remains a perennial problem in the region.
He was a perennial favorite to win the award.

meanings.definition: Of a plant, living for more than two years.
examples: Roses are perennial and bloom each summer.

meanings.definition: To corrupt morally; to make wicked.
examples: Critics claimed the films would deprave young viewers.
Power had depraved him beyond recognition.

meanings.definition: To allow or endure something without interfering, even if you dislike it.
examples: I will not tolerate rude behavior in this class.
She tolerated the noise because the rent was cheap.

meanings.definition: To be able to endure a substance or condition without harm.
examples: These plants tolerate drought remarkably well.

meanings.definition: Able to be compared because of similar qualities; roughly equivalent.
examples: The two houses are similar in size and comparable in price.
No other player is comparable to her in skill.

meanings.definition: Feeling or showing anger at something seen as unjust or wrong.
examples: She was indignant at being blamed unfairly.
He gave an indignant reply to the insult.

meanings.definition: Of a fault or sin, minor and easily forgiven.
examples: Forgetting his name was a venial slip, not a real offense.
The church distinguishes venial sins from mortal ones.

meanings.definition: The quality of evoking a keen sense of sadness or emotion.
examples: The poignancy of the farewell brought tears to her eyes.
The photograph captured the poignancy of wartime separation.

meanings.definition: The state or period of being a student or pupil.
examples: During his pupilage he studied under a master painter.
Her years of pupilage prepared her for the bar.

meanings.definition: Relating to King Arthur and the legends surrounding him.
examples: The novel retells an Arthurian legend.
Camelot is central to Arthurian romance.

meanings.definition: The position or duties of a tutor or guardian.
examples: He held the tutorship of the young prince.
Under her tutorship the children flourished.

meanings.definition: Extremely hot, especially from the sun.
examples: They crossed the torrid desert at dawn to avoid the heat.
The torrid afternoon drove everyone indoors.

meanings.definition: Full of intense passion or emotion.
examples: The novel describes their torrid love affair.

meanings.definition: Extremely thorough, careful, and strict.
examples: The product passed a rigorous series of safety tests.
She follows a rigorous training schedule.

meanings.definition: Harsh or severe, as conditions.
examples: They endured the rigorous winters of the far north.

meanings.definition: Too strong to be defeated or overcome.
examples: The champion seemed invincible until that night.
Their defense looked invincible all season.

meanings.definition: An experienced person who guides and advises someone less experienced.
examples: Her mentor encouraged her to apply for the fellowship.
A good mentor can shape a young person's career.

meanings.definition: To act as a guide and adviser to someone.
examples: He mentored several junior engineers at the firm.

meanings.definition: One of the parts or components that make up a mixture or whole.
examples: Flour is the main ingredient in bread.
Trust is a key ingredient of a strong friendship.

meanings.definition: A narrow strip of wood used to fasten, support, or cover joints.
examples: He nailed a batten across the loose boards.
The sail was stiffened with thin battens.

meanings.definition: To fasten or secure with strips of wood, as before a storm.
examples: The crew battened down the hatches before the gale.

meanings.definition: The state of being only partly or carelessly dressed.
examples: She answered the door in a state of dishabille.
He received guests in casual dishabille.

meanings.definition: The practice of magic, especially using supposed supernatural powers.
examples: She was accused of witchcraft and put on trial.
Folk tales are full of witchcraft and spells.

meanings.definition: An introductory statement that explains what follows.
examples: The preamble to the constitution states its purpose.
After a long preamble, he finally got to the point.

meanings.definition: The act of making amends for a wrong, injury, or loss.
examples: He offered money as reparation for the damage.
The nation paid reparations after the war.

meanings.definition: To change something slightly, especially to improve it.
examples: They modified the design to cut costs.
You can modify the recipe to suit your taste.

meanings.definition: A violent, lawless, or brutal person.
examples: A gang of ruffians attacked the travelers.
The ruffian threatened anyone who came near.

meanings.definition: The quality of being universal in scope or widely accepted.
examples: The catholicity of his tastes spanned every art form.
They admired the catholicity of her sympathies.

meanings.definition: To make one or more holes through something.
examples: The machine perforates the paper so it tears cleanly.
A nail perforated the tire.

meanings.definition: To dig out and remove earth or material to form a hole or hollow.
examples: Workers excavated the site for the new foundation.
Archaeologists excavated the ancient tomb.

meanings.definition: A strong, sudden forward surge or rush.
examples: The onrush of water swept away the bridge.
He braced himself against the onrush of the crowd.

meanings.definition: Unwillingness or hesitation to do something.
examples: She agreed only with great reluctance.
His reluctance to speak made the others suspicious.

meanings.definition: The state of being apart from others; privacy or solitude.
examples: The writer lived in seclusion for years.
They sought the seclusion of a remote cabin.

meanings.definition: To free someone from a false belief by telling them the truth.
examples: I must undeceive you about his real intentions.
She undeceived him before he made a costly mistake.

meanings.definition: Doing something for pleasure rather than as a paid profession.
examples: He is an amateur photographer with real talent.
The tournament is open to amateur players only.

meanings.definition: A person who does something for enjoyment rather than as a job.
examples: The race attracts both professionals and amateurs.

meanings.definition: Done routinely and without real care, interest, or effort.
examples: He gave the report only a perfunctory glance.
Her thanks were polite but perfunctory.

meanings.definition: Sudden and unexpected.
examples: The meeting came to an abrupt end.
There was an abrupt change in the weather.

meanings.definition: Curt or blunt in manner or speech.
examples: His abrupt reply left her feeling hurt.

meanings.definition: So small or unimportant as to be not worth considering.
examples: The cost difference between the two plans is negligible.
The damage to the car was negligible.

meanings.definition: Too great or intense to be expressed in words.
examples: She felt an unutterable joy at the reunion.
The scene filled them with unutterable horror.

meanings.definition: A means of securing the necessities of life; the way a person earns money to live.
examples: Fishing has been the family's livelihood for generations.
The drought threatened the livelihood of thousands of farmers.

meanings.definition: A clouding of the lens of the eye that causes partial or complete loss of vision.
examples: The surgeon removed the cataract and restored her sight.
Cataracts often develop gradually as people age.

meanings.definition: A large, steep waterfall or a sudden rush of water.
examples: The river plunged over the cliff in a thundering cataract.
Mist rose from the base of the cataract.

meanings.definition: The state of being unimportant or too small to matter.
examples: Standing beneath the mountains, he felt his own insignificance.
The error faded into insignificance once the project succeeded.

meanings.definition: To officially cancel a law or rule so that it is no longer in effect.
examples: Lawmakers voted to repeal the outdated tax.
The community campaigned to repeal the ban.

meanings.definition: Relating to or marked by formal ceremony or ritual.
examples: The chief wore a ceremonial robe for the festival.
They performed a ceremonial dance to open the games.

meanings.definition: Skilled at coming up with new and original ideas or devices.
examples: Her inventive mind solved problems no one else could.
The children found inventive ways to reuse old boxes.

meanings.definition: A small, showy trinket or ornament of little real value.
examples: She bought a cheap bauble at the market stall.
The tree was covered in glittering baubles.

meanings.definition: A false statement made to damage someone's reputation; slander.
examples: He sued the newspaper for spreading calumny about him.
The campaign sank into calumny and lies.

meanings.definition: Feeling, showing, or expressing compassion and understanding for others.
examples: The nurse gave the patient a sympathetic smile.
She was sympathetic to their financial struggles.

meanings.definition: Inclined to support or approve of something.
examples: The senator was sympathetic to the proposed reforms.
He found a sympathetic audience for his ideas.

meanings.definition: A change in the structure or form of something.
examples: The language underwent a gradual mutation over centuries.
Engineers tracked every mutation in the design.

meanings.definition: A change in the genetic material of a cell or organism.
examples: A random mutation gave the bacteria resistance to the drug.
Scientists study how each mutation affects the protein.

meanings.definition: To steal goods from a place, typically during a war or riot.
examples: Rioters looted shops along the main street.
Soldiers were forbidden to loot the captured town.

meanings.definition: Goods stolen or taken by force, especially during disorder or war.
examples: The thieves hid the loot in an abandoned barn.
Police recovered the stolen loot from the warehouse.

meanings.definition: Derived from the name of one's father or a male ancestor.
examples: In many cultures a patronymic surname signals the father's first name.
The patronymic ending in his name means 'son of Ivan'.

meanings.definition: A name derived from the name of one's father or ancestor.
examples: Russians traditionally use a patronymic alongside their given name.
His patronymic honored his grandfather.

meanings.definition: Impossible to make smaller, simpler, or to break down into parts.
examples: The budget had been cut to an irreducible minimum.
Some problems contain an irreducible amount of risk.

meanings.definition: The belief that God and the universe (including nature) are identical, or the worship of nature as divine.
examples: The poet's verses are steeped in a kind of pantheism.
Pantheism sees the divine in every part of the natural world.

meanings.definition: Doing or producing good; kind and generous.
examples: The beneficent donor funded the new hospital wing.
Her beneficent influence transformed the whole village.

meanings.definition: To cause severe and widespread damage to something.
examples: The storm ravaged the coastal towns.
Disease ravaged the population for years.

meanings.definition: To grant or bestow a title, honor, or right on someone.
examples: The university conferred an honorary degree on her.
Citizenship confers certain rights and duties.

meanings.definition: To discuss something with others in order to reach a decision.
examples: The jurors conferred for several hours before the verdict.
She stepped aside to confer with her lawyer.

meanings.definition: Based on facts and not influenced by personal feelings or opinions.
examples: A good journalist tries to give an objective account.
The judge weighed the objective evidence.

meanings.definition: A goal or aim that one is trying to achieve.
examples: Our main objective is to reduce waste.
The team met every objective on schedule.

meanings.definition: Showing an unhealthy or excessive interest in disturbing subjects such as death and disease.
examples: He had a morbid fascination with old graveyards.
The film dwells on morbid details.

meanings.definition: To fill someone with intense love or admiration (usually as 'enamored of').
examples: He was utterly enamored of the seaside town.
She became enamored with the idea of traveling alone.

meanings.definition: Remarkable or extraordinary; far beyond what is usual.
examples: The young pianist showed phenomenal talent.
Sales grew at a phenomenal rate.

meanings.definition: The use of sharp, often ironic remarks meant to mock or convey contempt.
examples: "Nice work," she said, her voice dripping with sarcasm.
His sarcasm hurt more than plain criticism would have.

meanings.definition: To make of brass or decorate with brass.
examples: Craftsmen would braze the small fittings by hand.
The lamp was brazed to give it a golden sheen.

meanings.definition: To join two pieces of metal by melting a filler metal between them.
examples: The technician brazed the copper pipes together.
They brazed the joint instead of welding it.

meanings.definition: Unable to express oneself clearly or fluently in words.
examples: Nervousness left him inarticulate during the interview.
She was so shocked she became briefly inarticulate.

meanings.definition: To make someone poor or to drain something of its strength or richness.
examples: Years of war impoverished the nation.
Over-farming impoverished the once-fertile soil.

meanings.definition: A connected series of military operations aimed at a particular goal.
examples: The general planned a winter campaign in the north.
The campaign drove the enemy back across the river.

meanings.definition: An organized series of actions to achieve a social, political, or commercial goal.
examples: She led a campaign to clean up the local river.
The advertising campaign boosted sales sharply.

meanings.definition: Pale, thin, and unhealthy in appearance, like a dead body.
examples: His cadaverous face frightened the children.
Weeks of illness had given her a cadaverous look.

meanings.definition: Splendid and expensive-looking; lavish.
examples: They served a sumptuous feast at the wedding.
The hotel's sumptuous suites overlooked the bay.

meanings.definition: A lack of belief or faith, especially in religion.
examples: She shook her head in unbelief at the news.
The sermon warned against doubt and unbelief.

meanings.definition: Something granted or given up, especially in response to a demand or claim.
examples: The union won several concessions from management.
Offering a discount was a small concession to keep the client.

meanings.definition: A right granted by a government or company to use land or run a business.
examples: The firm secured a mining concession in the region.
She runs the food concession at the stadium.

meanings.definition: Being the only one of its kind; unlike anything else.
examples: Each snowflake has a unique pattern.
Her voice is instantly recognizable and truly unique.

meanings.definition: To recognize or draw a clear distinction between things.
examples: A good taster can discriminate between subtle flavors.
The test measures your ability to discriminate among colors.

meanings.definition: To treat a person or group unfairly because of their characteristics.
examples: The law forbids employers to discriminate on the basis of age.
She felt the rules discriminated against newcomers.

meanings.definition: Not able to be set on fire or burned.
examples: The vault was built from incombustible materials.
Asbestos was once valued because it is incombustible.

meanings.definition: Having an exaggerated sense of one's own importance or abilities.
examples: His arrogant remarks annoyed the whole committee.
She was too arrogant to admit she was wrong.

meanings.definition: Too bad to be justified or forgiven.
examples: Arriving three hours late was inexcusable.
His rudeness to the guests was inexcusable.

meanings.definition: The substance or substances from which something is made or can be made.
examples: The bridge was built from the strongest material available.
She gathered material for her new dress.

meanings.definition: Information or ideas used as a basis for a work such as a book or speech.
examples: The reporter collected material for her article.
He drew material for the novel from his own childhood.

meanings.definition: To change something completely in form, nature, or substance.
examples: Alchemists hoped to transmute lead into gold.
Time transmuted his anger into quiet acceptance.

meanings.definition: To preserve or cherish something as sacred or worthy of lasting respect.
examples: These rights are enshrined in the constitution.
Her memory is enshrined in the hearts of the townspeople.

meanings.definition: A place where things are stored or kept, especially in large quantities.
examples: The archive serves as a repository for old maps.
The library is a vast repository of knowledge.

meanings.definition: A member of a gang that lives by robbery and plunder, especially in remote areas.
examples: Brigands ambushed the merchants on the mountain road.
The traveler feared an attack by brigands.

meanings.definition: To seize someone's property to force payment of a debt or obligation.
examples: The landlord threatened to distrain the tenant's furniture.
Officials may distrain goods to recover unpaid taxes.

meanings.definition: Inclined to agree with others or obey rules; yielding.
examples: The compliant child followed every instruction.
A compliant workforce rarely questions management.

meanings.definition: Meeting the requirements of a standard, law, or regulation.
examples: The new building is fully compliant with safety codes.
Make sure the software is compliant with privacy laws.

meanings.definition: Generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival or less powerful person.
examples: She was magnanimous in victory, praising her opponent.
It was magnanimous of him to forgive the debt.

meanings.definition: Deeply religious and devoted to one's faith.
examples: Her devout grandmother prayed every morning.
He was a devout follower of the tradition.

meanings.definition: Sincerely and earnestly committed to something.
examples: She is a devout supporter of the arts.
It was his devout hope that peace would return.

meanings.definition: The act or practice of bringing goods into one country from another.
examples: The importation of exotic fruit has grown rapidly.
New rules restricted the importation of foreign cars.

meanings.definition: An aromatic, oily resin used as a soothing or healing preparation.
examples: She rubbed a fragrant balsam on the wound.
The balsam eased his aching muscles.

meanings.definition: Able to use both hands equally well.
examples: The ambidextrous pitcher could throw with either arm.
Being ambidextrous, she wrote with whichever hand was free.

meanings.definition: To have casual or flirtatious romantic affairs, especially without serious intent.
examples: He spent his youth philandering instead of settling down.
She refused to marry a man who would philander.

meanings.definition: To obtain something by force, threats, or other unfair pressure.
examples: The gang tried to extort money from local shopkeepers.
He extorted a confession through intimidation.

meanings.definition: Sharing a common border; touching or next to each other.
examples: The two farms are contiguous along the river.
The states lie contiguous to one another.

meanings.definition: A word that sounds the same as another but differs in meaning, and sometimes spelling.
examples: "Bat" the animal and "bat" the club are homonyms.
"Bear" and "bare" are homonyms that confuse new readers.

meanings.definition: A symbol of abundance shaped like a horn overflowing with fruit and flowers.
examples: The painting featured a cornucopia spilling autumn fruit.
A cornucopia decorated the harvest table.

meanings.definition: An abundant supply of good things.
examples: The festival offered a cornucopia of music and food.
The library is a cornucopia of rare books.

meanings.definition: A pot that brews coffee by repeatedly cycling boiling water through the grounds.
examples: The percolator bubbled on the stove all morning.
She filled the percolator and waited for the coffee.

meanings.definition: The roof of the mouth.
examples: The hot soup burned the roof of her palate.
He pressed his tongue against his palate.

meanings.definition: A person's sense of taste or ability to appreciate flavors.
examples: The chef created a dish to delight every palate.
Fine wine appeals to a refined palate.

meanings.definition: A smooth-skinned variety of peach.
examples: She bit into a ripe, juicy nectarine.
The orchard grows both peaches and nectarines.

meanings.definition: The act of getting rid of something undesirable.
examples: The riddance of the old debts was a great relief.
"Good riddance," she said as the rude guest left.

meanings.definition: To smother or suppress something so it cannot develop or be expressed.
examples: He tried to stifle a yawn during the meeting.
Strict rules can stifle creativity.

meanings.definition: The study of language, especially in its historical and literary development.
examples: She devoted her career to the philology of ancient Greek.
Philology traces how words change over centuries.

meanings.definition: The people employed in an organization or engaged in a service.
examples: All personnel must wear identification badges.
The airline trained its personnel in safety procedures.

meanings.definition: The part of the body containing the digestive organs; the belly.
examples: The doctor pressed gently on her abdomen.
He felt a sharp pain in his lower abdomen.

meanings.definition: Widely and unfavorably known for something bad.
examples: The street was notorious for its traffic jams.
He was a notorious gambler.

meanings.definition: A state of agreement or harmony.
examples: Their views were in perfect concordance.
The treaty brought a rare concordance among the nations.

meanings.definition: An alphabetical index of the words used in a text, showing where they occur.
examples: She used a concordance to find every mention of the word.
The scholar compiled a concordance to the poems.

meanings.definition: The quality of being far too great or excessive, especially in amount or price.
examples: The exorbitance of the rent shocked the new tenants.
Critics condemned the exorbitance of his spending.

meanings.definition: An animal that walks on two feet.
examples: Humans are the most familiar bipeds.
The dinosaur was a fast-moving biped.

meanings.definition: Worth remembering or easily remembered because of being special or striking.
examples: The trip gave us many memorable moments.
It was a memorable performance that no one would forget.

meanings.definition: The act of keeping someone or something safe from harm or danger.
examples: The helmet offers protection against head injuries.
They sought protection from the storm in a cave.

meanings.definition: A duct or pipe in a chimney that carries away smoke and gases.
examples: Smoke rose up the flue and out the chimney.
The blocked flue filled the room with smoke.

meanings.definition: A woman's light, loose dressing gown, often made of sheer fabric.
examples: She slipped a silk negligee over her shoulders.
A lace negligee hung on the back of the door.

meanings.definition: Willing to allow or overlook the desires and faults of oneself or others.
examples: His indulgent parents rarely said no to him.
She gave an indulgent smile at the child's antics.

meanings.definition: Clearly visible or attracting notice; standing out.
examples: The bright sign was conspicuous from across the street.
Her absence at the meeting was conspicuous.

meanings.definition: An instrument that continuously records atmospheric pressure on a graph.
examples: The weather station's barograph traced a steady fall in pressure before the storm.
Sailors once relied on a barograph to anticipate sudden changes in the weather.

meanings.definition: A public street or road, especially a main one that runs through a place.
examples: The market spilled out onto the busy thoroughfare.
Traffic clogged the city's main thoroughfare during rush hour.

meanings.definition: Belonging to something as a natural and essential part of its nature.
examples: Honesty was an intrinsic part of her character.
Gold has an intrinsic value that does not depend on government decree.

meanings.definition: To gather or crowd together into a mass, blocking normal flow.
examples: Holiday travelers congest the highways every December.
A heavy cold can congest the nasal passages overnight.

meanings.definition: An excess of weight beyond what is normal, allowed, or required.
examples: The shipping company charged a fee for the parcel's overweight.
There was a slight overweight on the scale that the inspector noted.

meanings.definition: Weighing more than is normal, allowed, or healthy.
examples: The doctor warned that he was becoming overweight.
The overweight suitcase cost extra at the airline counter.

meanings.definition: A building equipped for systematic observation of the stars and other astronomical phenomena.
examples: Astronomers gathered at the mountaintop observatory to view the eclipse.
The observatory's huge telescope can capture light from distant galaxies.

meanings.definition: A person or thing of little or no importance or significance.
examples: Before his sudden fame, he had been a complete nonentity in politics.
The committee dismissed the proposal as the work of a nonentity.

meanings.definition: A stone coffin or chest-like tomb, often decorated with carvings.
examples: The pharaoh's mummy lay inside an elaborately carved sarcophagus.
Archaeologists pried open the ancient sarcophagus with great care.

meanings.definition: Using harsh, insulting language or cruel treatment.
examples: She finally left her abusive partner.
The referee ejected the player for abusive remarks.

meanings.definition: Using too many words; tediously long and wordy.
examples: His prolix lectures left the students yawning.
The report was so prolix that few readers reached the end.

meanings.definition: An official who supervises a game or contest and enforces its rules.
examples: The referee blew the whistle for a foul.
Players argued with the referee over the disputed call.

meanings.definition: A person who gives a reference about an applicant's character or abilities.
examples: She listed her former manager as a referee on the job application.
The committee contacted his referees before making an offer.

meanings.definition: Deserving blame for having done something wrong.
examples: The court found the driver culpable for the accident.
He felt culpable for the team's failure.

meanings.definition: Cutting, especially dividing something into two parts.
examples: A secant line passes through the curve at two distinct points.
The geometer drew a secant plane slicing the cone in two.

meanings.definition: A straight line that intersects a curve at two or more points.
examples: The secant crossed the circle at points A and B.
In trigonometry, the secant of an angle is the reciprocal of its cosine.

meanings.definition: The means or resources, especially money, needed for a purpose.
examples: He lacked the wherewith to pay for a college education.
They finally had the wherewith to repair the old house.

meanings.definition: To regard with deep respect and reverence.
examples: Many cultures venerate their ancestors.
Students came to venerate the wise old teacher.

meanings.definition: The office, rank, or term of office of an alderman.
examples: He held the aldermanship of the ward for nearly a decade.
Her election to the aldermanship marked the start of a long civic career.

meanings.definition: The act of spending money or other resources, or the cost involved.
examples: They spared no expense in planning the wedding.
The trip was a major expense for the family.

meanings.definition: Showing keen judgment and wise discernment.
examples: The sagacious investor sold her shares before the crash.
His sagacious advice steered the company through hard times.

meanings.definition: To flow out or come forth from a source.
examples: A warm light seemed to emanate from the candle.
Confidence emanated from the new leader.

meanings.definition: A condition of dependence on alcohol resulting from its excessive and habitual use.
examples: Years of heavy drinking led to his alcoholism.
She sought treatment for alcoholism at a recovery center.

meanings.definition: Easily affected, changed, or influenced by outside conditions.
examples: Photographic film is sensitive to light.
The instrument is sensitive to the slightest vibration.

meanings.definition: Readily responsive to or affected by the feelings of oneself or others.
examples: She is sensitive to criticism.
He was sensitive enough to notice his friend's sadness.

meanings.definition: Full of sorrow; expressing or causing sadness.
examples: The dog gave a doleful howl when its owner left.
A doleful melody drifted from the old piano.

meanings.definition: A person who pleads the cause of another, especially in a court of law.
examples: She hired an advocate to argue her case.
He became a tireless advocate for the poor.

meanings.definition: To publicly support or recommend a particular cause or policy.
examples: The senator continued to advocate for clean energy.
Doctors advocate regular exercise for a healthy heart.

meanings.definition: Exposed to too little light to develop properly, as a photographic image.
examples: The underexposed photo came out dark and murky.
Several frames were underexposed because the shutter was too fast.

meanings.definition: The quality of being holy, sacred, or deserving deep respect.
examples: The pilgrims honored the sanctity of the shrine.
Many people defend the sanctity of human life.

meanings.definition: Marked by bitterness and sharp hostility.
examples: The couple went through an acrimonious divorce.
The debate turned acrimonious as tempers flared.

meanings.definition: Not showing care or good judgment for the future; rash.
examples: It was imprudent to invest all his savings in one stock.
She made an imprudent remark she soon regretted.

meanings.definition: The act of persuading someone not to do something.
examples: Despite their dissuasion, he went ahead with the risky plan.
Her gentle dissuasion kept him from quitting his job.

meanings.definition: Of or like brothers; showing warmth and friendship.
examples: The two old friends shared a fraternal bond.
He joined a fraternal organization devoted to charity.

meanings.definition: Self-governing; acting or existing independently.
examples: The region became an autonomous province.
Autonomous vehicles can drive without a human at the wheel.

meanings.definition: To pay or repay someone for a service, loss, or injury.
examples: The company agreed to compensate workers for overtime.
Insurance will compensate them for the flood damage.

meanings.definition: To offset or make up for a deficiency or weakness.
examples: He compensated for his lack of height with great speed.
Extra practice helped compensate for her late start.

meanings.definition: A person devoted to luxury and self-indulgent pleasure.
examples: The wealthy sybarite filled his villa with silk and fine wine.
Living like a sybarite, he never rose before noon.

meanings.definition: The act of rising or climbing upward.
examples: The balloon's slow ascension thrilled the crowd.
His ascension to the throne came after his father's death.

meanings.definition: Causing harm or damage, physically or morally.
examples: Smoking has a deleterious effect on the lungs.
The policy proved deleterious to small businesses.

meanings.definition: An opening, hole, or gap, especially one that lets light through.
examples: Light streamed through a narrow aperture in the wall.
Widening the camera's aperture lets in more light.

meanings.definition: To feel deep regret or remorse over something.
examples: He lived to rue the day he sold the family farm.
She rued her hasty words almost at once.

meanings.definition: Narrow or unsophisticated in outlook and manner.
examples: His provincial views made him uneasy in the cosmopolitan city.
They mocked her provincial taste in clothes.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to a province or the regions outside the capital.
examples: The provincial government raised local taxes.
She grew up in a quiet provincial town.

meanings.definition: A driving force or stimulus that encourages a process or action.
examples: The scandal gave fresh impetus to calls for reform.
Falling prices provided the impetus for buyers to act.

meanings.definition: A long, angry speech of criticism or condemnation.
examples: He launched into a tirade against the new regulations.
Her tirade about the poor service silenced the whole room.

meanings.definition: Subject to a tax, especially a customs duty.
examples: Imported wine is a dutiable item at the border.
Travelers must declare all dutiable goods to customs.

meanings.definition: Continuing without pause or interruption.
examples: The incessant rain flooded the streets.
Her incessant chatter made it hard to concentrate.

meanings.definition: The condition of being a slave or being forced to obey another.
examples: The captives were sold into servitude.
They struggled for years to escape political servitude.

meanings.definition: Something written in code or with a hidden, secret meaning.
examples: The spy concealed the orders inside a clever cryptogram.
Puzzle fans enjoy decoding a tricky cryptogram.

meanings.definition: The act of deliberately confusing or bewildering someone.
examples: The magician's tricks were pure mystification.
His vague answers were a deliberate mystification of the facts.

meanings.definition: Making a hypocritical show of being morally superior or pious.
examples: His sanctimonious lecture annoyed everyone at the table.
She gave a sanctimonious smile while criticizing her neighbors.

meanings.definition: A structure or support that props up or strengthens something.
examples: A stone buttress kept the cathedral wall from collapsing.
Family was the emotional buttress that held her up.

meanings.definition: To support, strengthen, or reinforce.
examples: New evidence helped buttress their argument.
Thick walls buttress the old fortress.

meanings.definition: The condition of being normal or ordinary.
examples: After the storm, life slowly returned to normalcy.
They longed for a sense of normalcy amid the chaos.

meanings.definition: The office, position, or term of the pope as head of the Roman Catholic Church.
examples: His papacy lasted nearly twenty years.
Reforms made during her uncle's papacy reshaped the church.

meanings.definition: A way or passage out of a place.
examples: The crowd rushed toward the nearest exit.
An illuminated sign marked the emergency exit.

meanings.definition: To go out of or leave a place.
examples: Please exit through the rear doors.
The actor exited the stage to loud applause.

meanings.definition: Swollen, enlarged blood vessels in or around the lower rectum and anus.
examples: The doctor prescribed a cream to ease his hemorrhoids.
Straining too hard can lead to painful hemorrhoids.

meanings.definition: A small mistake or slight deviation from what is right or expected.
examples: A momentary lapse in concentration cost him the match.
She apologized for her lapse in judgment.

meanings.definition: To gradually fall or slip into a worse or former state.
examples: He lapsed into silence after the bad news.
Her membership lapsed when she forgot to renew it.

meanings.definition: A blunt or sudden rejection of an offer, request, or advance.
examples: His friendly offer met with a cold rebuff.
She was stung by the rebuff from the committee.

meanings.definition: To reject or refuse someone or something in an abrupt way.
examples: The company rebuffed all attempts at a takeover.
He rebuffed her invitation without a word of thanks.

meanings.definition: An amount of something that is more than is needed; an excess.
examples: There was a superfluity of food left after the banquet.
The essay suffered from a superfluity of needless detail.

meanings.definition: Something that blocks or hinders progress or movement.
examples: A fallen tree was an obstruction on the road.
The lawyer was charged with obstruction of justice.

meanings.definition: To frighten or threaten someone, especially to make them do something.
examples: They tried to intimidate the witness into staying silent.
Don't let his loud voice intimidate you.

meanings.definition: A person who is responsible for a crime or wrongdoing.
examples: Police soon caught the culprit.
A faulty wire was the culprit behind the fire.

meanings.definition: With the head leading the way; headlong.
examples: He dived head foremost into the pool.
The child tumbled head foremost down the slide.

meanings.definition: Relating to the planet Mars or to the Roman god of war.
examples: Scientists study Martian soil for signs of past water.
The probe sent back stunning Martian landscapes.

meanings.definition: An imagined inhabitant of the planet Mars.
examples: The old film featured green Martians invading Earth.
Children dressed as Martians for the costume party.

meanings.definition: A ghost or ghostly apparition.
examples: A pale specter was said to haunt the castle halls.
The legend told of a specter wandering the moor at night.

meanings.definition: A haunting or threatening possibility of something bad.
examples: The specter of famine loomed over the drought-stricken land.
Investors feared the specter of another recession.

meanings.definition: Too powerful or tempting to be resisted or opposed.
examples: The smell of fresh bread was irresistible.
An irresistible force swept the army forward.

meanings.definition: A device that breaks a liquid into a fine spray.
examples: She spritzed perfume from a small glass atomizer.
The atomizer dispersed the medicine as a fine mist.

meanings.definition: A medieval forerunner of chemistry concerned chiefly with turning base metals into gold.
examples: Medieval alchemy sought the secret of transforming lead into gold.
His laboratory looked like a den of ancient alchemy.

meanings.definition: A seemingly magical process of transforming something ordinary into something special.
examples: By some alchemy, she turned leftovers into a feast.
The director worked his usual alchemy on a weak script.

meanings.definition: Polite and considerate behavior toward others.
examples: He treated every guest with great courtesy.
As a courtesy, the hotel offered free breakfast.

meanings.definition: A court officer who keeps order and has custody of prisoners.
examples: The bailiff led the defendant into the courtroom.
Order was restored when the bailiff stepped forward.

meanings.definition: A very steep or vertical cliff face.
examples: The hikers peered cautiously over the precipice.
The path wound along the edge of a dizzying precipice.

meanings.definition: The brink of a dangerous or disastrous situation.
examples: The nation stood on the precipice of war.
The company was on the precipice of bankruptcy.

meanings.definition: The art and practice of preparing and enjoying fine food.
examples: France is famous for its rich gastronomy.
The festival celebrated regional gastronomy with dozens of dishes.

meanings.definition: An official in charge of funds at a college or other institution; a treasurer.
examples: Students paid their tuition to the bursar.
The bursar managed the school's entire budget.

meanings.definition: A teacher of the highest rank at a university or college.
examples: The professor lectured on ancient Roman history.
She was promoted to full professor of physics.

meanings.definition: The act of entering or moving into something.
examples: The river's slow introgression reshaped the valley floor.
Scientists traced the introgression of new genes into the population.

meanings.definition: A typically hard, shiny, heavy element that conducts heat and electricity and forms a base with oxygen.
examples: Iron is a common and useful metal.
The bridge was built from steel and other metals.

meanings.definition: The ability to understand and grasp the meaning of something.
examples: Her reading comprehension improved dramatically over the semester.
The instructions were beyond his comprehension.

meanings.definition: A strong feeling that something, usually unpleasant, is about to happen.
examples: She had a premonition that the trip would end badly.
He couldn't shake the premonition of disaster.

meanings.definition: Adding emphasis or force to a word or expression.
examples: The word 'very' is used as an intensive to strengthen the adjective.

meanings.definition: Concentrated and thorough, involving a great deal of effort in a short time.
examples: The patient was placed in intensive care.
She took an intensive course in Spanish.

meanings.definition: Able to be conveyed or moved from one person or place to another.
examples: The ticket is not transferable to another person.
These skills are transferable to almost any job.

meanings.definition: The act of removing someone from a job or position by authority.
examples: His dismissal from the company came as a shock.
The workers protested the unfair dismissal.

meanings.definition: The act of treating something as unworthy of serious consideration.
examples: Her casual dismissal of his concerns hurt him.

meanings.definition: An unbroken and consistent connection or continuation over space, time, or development.
examples: There is a clear continuity between the old and new policies.
The film editor checked for continuity between scenes.

meanings.definition: Unwise or not showing good judgment in dealing with others.
examples: It would be impolitic to criticize the host at his own party.
His impolitic remarks offended several guests.

meanings.definition: Stretched or pulled tight; not slack.
examples: The rope was pulled taut between the two posts.
Her muscles grew taut with tension.

meanings.definition: Tense or characterized by emotional strain.
examples: The thriller kept the audience in a state of taut suspense.

meanings.definition: The office, rank, or function of a magistrate.
examples: He was elevated to the magistracy after years of legal service.
The magistracy held considerable power in the ancient city.

meanings.definition: To offer something to a deity, especially by presenting it on an altar.
examples: The priests would sacrifice a lamb at the temple.
Ancient peoples sacrificed animals to please their gods.

meanings.definition: To give up something valued for the sake of something considered more important.
examples: She sacrificed her free time to care for her family.
He sacrificed his career to support his children.

meanings.definition: Impossible to hold back, conceal, or restrain.
examples: She felt an insuppressible urge to laugh during the ceremony.
His insuppressible enthusiasm spread to the whole team.

meanings.definition: A firm denial of knowledge, responsibility, or connection.
examples: The government issued a disavowal of any involvement in the plot.
His disavowal of the rumor did little to calm the public.

meanings.definition: A swift and spirited horse.
examples: The knight rode a powerful courser into battle.
Her courser galloped across the open field.

meanings.definition: The throwing back of light, heat, sound, or other wave energy from a surface.
examples: The reflection of the moon shimmered on the lake.
Sunglasses reduce the glare from reflection off the water.

meanings.definition: Serious thought or consideration about something.
examples: After much reflection, she decided to accept the job.

meanings.definition: An image seen in a mirror or other shiny surface.
examples: He stared at his reflection in the window.

meanings.definition: To make a piece of writing or speech shorter and more concise.
examples: She condensed her long report into a single page.
The author condensed the novel into a short story.

meanings.definition: To change from a gas or vapor into a liquid.
examples: Steam condenses into water on the cold window.
Moisture condensed on the outside of the glass.

meanings.definition: A young cow that has not yet had a calf.
examples: The farmer raised a heifer to add to his dairy herd.
A spotted heifer grazed in the meadow.

meanings.definition: Writing done by hand; a person's particular style of forming letters.
examples: Her neat handwriting was easy to read.
The teacher could recognize each student by their handwriting.

meanings.definition: To award, grant, or decide formally by a judicial or official decision.
examples: The court adjudged him guilty of the offense.
The prize was adjudged to the most original entry.

meanings.definition: Careful and prudent in speech or action, especially to avoid causing offense or revealing private information.
examples: She was discreet about her friend's secret.
He made a few discreet inquiries before deciding.

meanings.definition: To drive or slide together so that one part fits inside another, like the sections of a spyglass.
examples: The cars telescoped into one another during the crash.
The collapsible pole telescopes down for easy storage.

meanings.definition: To compress or condense something into a shorter form or time.
examples: The series telescoped years of history into a single episode.
